description: Application service implements use-case interfaces (inbound ports) and depends only on outbound ports, never on infrastructure — the hallmark of clean architecture's application layer.
OAuthClientPort is an outbound port (driven port) in hexagonal architecture, abstracting external OAuth provider communication so the application core stays infrastructure-agnostic.
UserRepositoryAdapter is a driven adapter that implements the UserRepositoryPort interface, bridging domain port contracts with JPA infrastructure.
TokenAdapter implements TokenPort (outbound port), adapting the JJWT library implementation to the port interface defined in the application layer.
GoogleOAuthClient adapts the Google OAuth2 REST API to the internal OAuthClientPort interface, acting as a classic object adapter between external API and domain contract.

codeSnippet:
override suspend fun exchangeCodeForToken(authCode: String, redirectUri: String): OAuthTokenResult {
    val webClient = webClientBuilder.build()
    val response = webClient.post()
        .uri("https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token")
        .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_FORM_URLENCODED)
        .body(
            BodyInserters.fromFormData("grant_type", "authorization_code")
                .with("client_id", clientId)
                .with("client_secret", clientSecret)
                .with("redirect_uri", redirectUri)
description: Implements the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow — exchanging an authorization code for an access token from Google's token endpoint.

codeSnippet:
override suspend fun exchangeCodeForToken(authCode: String, redirectUri: String): OAuthTokenResult {
    val webClient = webClientBuilder.build()
    val response = webClient.post()
        .uri("https://kauth.kakao.com/oauth/token")
        .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_FORM_URLENCODED)
        .body(
            BodyInserters.fromFormData("grant_type", "authorization_code")
                .with("client_id", clientId)
                .with("client_secret", clientSecret)
                .with("redirect_uri", redirectUri)
description: Implements the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow for Kakao's identity provider, demonstrating multi-provider OAuth support.
Generates a signed JWT access token with subject, type claim, and expiry — implementing stateless authentication via HMAC-signed tokens.
Validates a refresh JWT by verifying the HMAC signature and checking the token type claim, returning the userId or null on any failure.
TokenPort defines a pure interface contract for token operations, decoupling use-cases from the concrete JWT implementation.

codeSnippet:
interface OAuthLoginUseCase {
    suspend fun execute(command: Command): Result

    data class Command(
        val provider: AuthProvider,
        val authCode: String,
        val redirectUri: String
    )

    data class Result(
        val accessToken: String,
        val refreshToken: String,
        val userId: Long,
        val isNewUser: Boolean
    )
}
description: Use-case interface bundles Command input and Result output as nested data classes, providing a self-contained use-case contract typical in clean/hexagonal architecture.
Uses Kotlin coroutines with suspend functions and .awaitSingle() to bridge reactive WebClient calls into coroutine-friendly async execution.

codeSnippet:
val response = webClient.post()
    .uri("https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token")
    .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_FORM_URLENCODED)
    .body(
        BodyInserters.fromFormData("grant_type", "authorization_code")
            ...
    )
    .retrieve()
    .bodyToMono(GoogleTokenResponse::class.java)
    .awaitSingle()
description: WebClient produces a Mono (reactive stream publisher) representing the async HTTP response, which is consumed by awaitSingle() in the coroutine context.

User is a rich domain aggregate with private constructor, factory methods (create/restore), invariant guards via require(), and a domain behavior method (updateProfile) — classic DDD aggregate root pattern.
Companion object static factory methods (create vs restore) enforce invariants and control object construction, implementing the Factory pattern with private constructor.

codeSnippet:
companion object {
    fun fromDomain(user: User) = UserJpaEntity(
        id = user.id,
        email = user.email,
        name = user.name,
        provider = user.provider,
        providerId = user.providerId,
        profileImageUrl = user.profileImageUrl,
        createdAt = user.createdAt
    )
}
description: Companion object hosts the fromDomain factory function for mapping from domain model to JPA entity, providing static-like utility scoped to the class.
Companion object provides a from() factory function to map use-case results to DTOs, a common Kotlin pattern for static-like construction methods.

codeSnippet:
data class OAuthTokenResult(val accessToken: String)

data class OAuthUserInfo(
    val email: String,
    val name: String,
    val providerId: String,
    val profileImageUrl: String?
)
description: Data classes model plain data transfer objects within the port layer, leveraging Kotlin's auto-generated equals/hashCode/copy/toString for immutable value carriers.
AuthProvider enum models the closed set of supported OAuth identity providers, enforcing type-safe provider selection throughout the domain.
DataSourceType enum represents the two routing targets (MASTER/REPLICA), used as type-safe keys in the AbstractRoutingDataSource map.
Domain-specific exception classes extend a common BusinessException with typed ErrorCode enums, allowing global exception handlers to map domain errors to HTTP responses uniformly.
JPA annotations map the UserJpaEntity class to the users table with auto-generated identity, enum column mapping, and unique constraint — demonstrating ORM object-relational mapping.
Multiple JPA and Hibernate annotations (@Entity, @Table, @Id, @GeneratedValue, @Enumerated, @CreationTimestamp) drive runtime behaviour through metadata rather than imperative code.
RESTful controller with resource-based URL design (/api/auth/login/{provider}), HTTP POST for login, JSON request/response bodies wrapped in a uniform ApiResponse envelope.
UserRepositoryPort abstracts persistence operations, exposing only domain-meaningful methods so the application layer never depends on JPA, SQL, or database details.
AuthService implements multiple use-case interfaces (polymorphism via interface), and OAuthClientPort is resolved polymorphically from a Map at runtime based on the provider enum.
Private constructor encapsulates User object creation, forcing all construction through factory methods (create/restore) that enforce business invariants before allowing instantiation.

The X-User-Id header is injected by the API gateway after JWT verification, illustrating the MSA pattern where the auth service trusts a trusted upstream header rather than re-validating tokens per request.
The SecretKey is lazily initialized once per TokenAdapter instance using Kotlin's by lazy delegate, ensuring the expensive key derivation happens only once (singleton-like initialization).

codeSnippet: ): ResponseEntity<ApiResponse<LoginResponse>> {
description: ResponseEntity<ApiResponse<LoginResponse>> uses nested generic type parameters to compose a typed HTTP response wrapper around a typed API envelope, providing compile-time type safety for response shapes.

Spring Security's DSL accepts lambda configurers ({ it.disable() }, { it.sessionCreationPolicy(...) }) as trailing lambda parameters, demonstrating Kotlin lambda syntax for builder-style configuration.
All endpoints are permitted without authentication (anyRequest().permitAll()), which allows load balancer health-check probes and actuator endpoints to pass without requiring a token.
